table_data.title: Trimsamsa Rulers in Odd Signs (Oja Rashi)
Trimsamsa Rulers in Even Signs (Yugma Rashi)

raw_text:
26
आसकपारिजाते
Adh I
But this interpretation presents certain difficulties. Apart
from syntactical objections, the compound Virinchi Saurisadivaka-
raha (विरिश्चिशौौरशिदिवाकराः) will not bear ainumerical interpretation
as neither the Phaladeepika (फलदीपिका) nor the Sarwarttha Chinta-
mani (सर्वार्थचिन्तामणिः), nor even Parasara (पराशर) varies the names of
the divinities into those of any other denomination .g. Nanda (नन्द),
Dik (दिक), and the like, as they could have done if they had the
Arnavadi Sankhya (अर्णवादिसंख्या) in view. If numbers were in their
mind, one or other of our ancient authors would have ordinary
Sankhya Vachaka Subanthaha (संख्यावाचकसुबन्ताः). It is unfortunate
that Balabhadra (बलभद्र), such a copious author in other respects,
does not so much as even mention the Shodasainsa (षोडशांश) in his
Horaratna (होरारल). Had he chosen to introduce the topic, it would
have put an end to all trouble.
The word Vilagne' (विलग्ने) in the second half of the sloka
should be read with Oje (ओजे) in first line also. Vilagne विलप्ने)
can only mean the 'rising sign'. The verse as a whole considers the
possibility of a 'rising sign' being 'positive, or negative, or male
or female, as they are termed in Western Astrology. The lines
quoted above from Parasara (पराशर) tend to confirm this view and
the interpretation as given in the translation is almost near the
mark. But Phaladeepika (फलदीपिका) and Sarwarttha Chintamani
(सर्वार्थचिन्तमणि) interpret the allocation of the Shodasamsa (पोडशांश)
rulerships diflerently. On that account, the language of Jataka-
parijata (जातकपारिजात) cannot be forced to bear a meaning which its
author did not evidently intend. Jatakaparijata (जातकपारिजात) and
Parasara (पराशर go a good way together as regards Shodasamsa
(षोडशांश) rulerships.
आरार्किजीवशशिनन्दनशुक्रभागा-
स्त्वोजे समीरपत्रनाष्टकरौलबाणाः ।
युग्मे समीरगिरिपश्नगपञ्चवाणा-
त्रिशांशकाः सितविदार्यशनिक्षमाजाः ॥ ३७ ॥
Sloka 37. The Thrimasmsas (विंशांश) or degrees, i.e.
oth portions allotted in an odd sign to Kuja, Sani.
Guru, Budha and Sukra are respectively 5, 5, 8, 7 and 5.
extraction_notes: The translation for Shloka 37 is incomplete as it is cut off at the bottom of the page.; The book appears to contain a typo in the translation of Shloka 37, using 'Thrimasmsas (विंशांश)' which means '20th division', instead of the correct 'Trimsamsas (त्रिंशांश)' which means '30th division' and is the topic of the shloka.; The Sanskrit number words in Shloka 37 appear non-standard or mis-transcribed in the source text, but the degree values given in the translation are standard and have been used for the derived tables.
